resources.annotation: Splits transparency between providers and deployers: providers design AI-interaction disclosure (¶1, unless obvious) and mark synthetic audio/image/video/text as machine-readable/artificially generated (¶2); deployers inform people exposed to emotion-recognition/biometric-categorisation (¶3), disclose deep fakes (¶4, artistic-works carve-out), and disclose AI-generated text published on matters of public interest (¶4, human-editorial carve-out). Extract which paragraph binds which role — the proactive-disclosure side of D4, distinct from Article 73 notification.

resources.title: EU AI Act — Article 53 (Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models)
resources.annotation: Upstream GPAI-provider duties that matter to a procuring deployer: keep technical documentation (¶1(a)); make information/documentation available to DOWNSTREAM providers so they understand the model's capabilities and limitations (¶1(b)); a copyright-compliance policy (¶1(c)); a public training-content summary per AI Office template (¶1(d)); with a carve-out for free/open-source models without systemic risk. Grounds the procurement point — insist the upstream documentation flows to you, but keep your own deployer duties.

resources.title: EU AI Act — Article 72 (Post-market monitoring by providers)
resources.annotation: Providers of high-risk systems must establish and document a post-market monitoring system, proportionate to risk, that actively and systematically collects, documents and analyses performance data throughout the system's lifetime, based on a post-market monitoring plan in the technical documentation (Commission template due 2 Feb 2026). Extract the 'monitoring is continuous, lifetime surveillance' framing that anchors D4's monitoring lesson and the drift scenario.

resources.title: EU AI Act — Article 73 (Reporting of serious incidents)
resources.annotation: Serious-incident notification to the market surveillance authority of the Member State where the incident occurred, on a tiered clock after becoming aware: 15 days general (¶2); 10 days if a person died (¶4); 2 days for widespread infringement or serious/irreversible disruption of critical infrastructure (¶3). Extract the 2/10/15-day ladder (most-severe = shortest window) and that it is distinct from Article 50 transparency — the notification side of D4.

resources.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework — Core (GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE)
resources.annotation: The voluntary AI RMF 1.0 core functions: GOVERN (culture + accountability structures; GOVERN 2 accountability, GOVERN 6 third-party risk), MAP (frame context), MEASURE (analyse/benchmark/monitor risk), and MANAGE (allocate resources to mapped/measured risks regularly; respond to, recover from and communicate about incidents; MANAGE 3 third-party risk; MANAGE 4 documented, regularly monitored response/recovery). For D4, MANAGE is the monitoring/incident function; GOVERN 2 anchors accountability. Also note the AI RMF Playbook and the Generative AI Profile (NIST-AI-600-1).

resources.title: OECD AI Principles
resources.annotation: The five values-based OECD AI Principles: inclusive growth/sustainable development/well-being; human rights and democratic values (incl. fairness and privacy); transparency and explainability; robustness, security and safety; and accountability. Extract the Accountability principle (1.5) — 'AI actors should be accountable for the proper functioning of AI systems and for the respect of the principles, based on their roles, the context, and consistent with the state of the art' — which underpins D4's named-owner accountability structures.

resources.title: EU AI Act — Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (official text, EUR-Lex)
resources.annotation: The authoritative consolidated legal text of the EU AI Act on EUR-Lex — the primary source of record for every Article cited in this domain (3, 4, 6, 25, 26, 27, 50, 53, 72, 73) and Annex III. Use it to confirm exact legal wording of any deployer obligation, transparency duty, or incident-reporting deadline before asserting it as fact.

resources.title: ISO/IEC 42001 — Artificial intelligence — Management system (AIMS)
resources.annotation: The AI management-system standard (AIMS) that provides the organisational wrapper for D4's accountability and operational controls — establishing, implementing, maintaining and continually improving governance of AI across the lifecycle, including supplier/third-party management. Folds in from D2 as the management-system backbone into which named accountabilities, monitoring, and incident processes slot. (Verify the exact scope wording directly on the ISO page — iso.org restricts automated fetching, so confirm at authoring time.)
